{{Short description|Liberal trend in Japanese society (1904–1926)}}
'''Taishō Democracy''' was a liberal and democratic trend across the political, economic, and cultural fields in Japan that began roughly after the [[Russo-Japanese War]] and continued until the end of the [[Taishō era]] (1912{{endash}}1926). This trend was most evident in the field of politics, famously represented by the {{nihongo|Taishō Democracy Movement|大正デモクラシー運動}} and the establishment of the {{nihongo|Seitō Naikaku System|政党内閣制}}, a [[representative democracy]] in which the party with a majority in [[:en:National Diet|parliament]] organizes the cabinet.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|title=Nihon no kin-gendaishi o dō miru ka|date=2010|publisher=Iwanami Shoten|others=Iwanami Shoten. Iwanami Shinsho Henshūbu., 岩波書店. 岩波新書編集部.|isbn=978-4-00-431051-8|location=Tōkyō|pages=81|oclc=534643529}}</ref> The term "Taishō Democracy" has been widely used since the book "{{nihongo|The History of Taishō Democracy|大正デモクラシー史|Taishō demokurashii Shi}}" written by [[Shinobu Seizaburō]].<ref>{{Cite book|last=Shinobu|first=Seizaburo|title=Taisho Democrashii shi.|publisher=Nippon Hyoron Sha|year=1954|location=Tokyo}}</ref>
== Summary ==
=== Establishment === Although there are many theories about when Taishō Democracy began and ended, 1905 is often said to be the starting year of the Democratic era. 1905 was the year when [[:en:Bloody Sunday (1905)|Bloody Sunday]] happened in Russia, which subsequently led to the [[Russian Revolution]].<ref>{{Cite web|last=日本大百科全書（ニッポニカ）|title=ロシア革命|url=https://japanknowledge.com|access-date=2020-08-01|website=JapanKnowledge}}</ref> Bloody Sunday was instigated by loss of faith in the Tsarist government as a result of public discontent with the results of the Russo-Japanese War. In other words, the combination of the burden on the workers and general populace and the remote and costly war against Japan contributed greatly to the public unrest which sparked the 1905 revolution.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Shinobu|first=Seizaburo|title=Taisho Democrashii shi|publisher=Nippon Hyoron Sha|year=1954|location=Tokyo|pages=3}}</ref> Meanwhile, a similar combination existed in Japan. During the war and even before the war, Japanese socialists stated that the war would need sacrifice from the people. [[:en:Heimin Shinbun|Heimin Shinbun]] pointed out on 14 February 1904 that "The people will suffer for a long time from taxation for the war.".<ref>{{Cite book|last=Shinobu|first=Seizaburo|title=Taisho Democrashii shi|publisher=Nippon Hyoron Sha|year=1954|location=Tokyo|pages=7}}</ref> Some Christians and women's liberationists expressed similar anti-war attitudes.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Shinobu|first=Seizaburo|title=Taisho Democrashii shi|publisher=Nippon Hyoron Sha|year=1954|location=Tokyo|pages=9}}</ref>
The Russo-Japanese war ended in 1905, and [[Treaty of Portsmouth|the peace treaty]] was signed. Although Japan won, the general populace was disappointed by the result of the peace treaty.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Shinobu|first=Seizaburo|title=Taisho Democrashii shi|publisher=Nippon Hyoron Sha|year=1954|location=Tokyo|pages=15}}</ref> The Japanese people saw the peace treaty, which lacked major Russian territorial cessions and monetary reparations, as foreshadowing more sacrifice without benefits. Public anger toward the government gradually grew, and eventually led to the foundation of the movement against the Russo-Japanese war peace treaty.
=== Key Events === There are many theories about the timing of the Taishō Democracy, but many historians agree that the following events are crucial to Taishō Democracy.
First of all, there was the Russo-Japanese War peace treaty protest movement and the [[:en:Hibiya incendiary incident|Hibiya incendiary incident]] that accompanied it.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Shinobu|first=Seizaburo|title=Taisho Democrashii Shi|publisher=Nippon Hyoron Sya|year=1954|location=Tokyo|pages=39}}</ref> The movement grew across the country and gradually exposed the people's dissatisfaction with their government. Over time, the movement transformed into the Movement to Protect Constitutional Government, thereby igniting the {{nihongo|[[Taishō political crisis]]|大正政変}}.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Shinobu|first=Seizaburo|title=Taisho Democrashii Shi|publisher=Nippon Hyoron Sha|year=1954|location=Tokyo|pages=232}}</ref> This was the first instance of a popular movement trying to replace the cabinet in Japanese history, and the [[:en:Katsura Tarō|Katsura]] cabinet collapsed only 53 days after its formation.<ref>{{Cite web|last=日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ)|title=大正デモクラシー|url=https://japanknowledge.com|website=JapanKnowledge}}</ref> {{nihongo|[[Rice riots of 1918|The rice riots of 1918]]|米騒動}} and the establishment of the [[:en:Hara Takashi|Hara]] Cabinet are considered to be a major milestone in Taisho democracy.<ref name=":0" /> When the rice riots subsided, the {{nihongo|universal suffrage movement|普選運動}} was in full swing and became a nationwide political movement.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Shinobu|first=Seizaburo|title=Taisho Demokurashii Shi|publisher=Nippon Hyoron Sya|year=1954|location=Tokyo|pages=505}}</ref> The {{nihongo|[[:en:General Election Law|general election law]]|普通選挙法}} was enacted under the subsequent cabinet of {{nihongo|[[:en:Katō Takaaki|Takaaki Kato]]|加藤高明}}.<ref>{{Cite web|last=日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ)|title=普通選挙法|url=https://japanknowledge.com|website=JapanKnowledge}}</ref> In 1932, {{nihongo|[[Rikken Seiyūkai]]|政友会}}, {{nihongo|[[:en:Kenseikai|Kenseikai]]|憲政会}}, and {{nihongo|[[:en:Kakushin Club|Kakushin Club]]|革新倶楽部}} launched a campaign to overthrow [[:en:Kiyoura Keigo|Kiyoura Keigo]]'s cabinet. The campaign grew into what came to be called the {{nihongo|Second Constitutional Movement|第二次護憲運動}}.<ref>{{Cite web|last=国史大辞典|title=憲政擁護運動|url=https://japanknowledge.com|website=JapanKnowledge}}</ref>
== Background ==
=== Minpon Shugi === [[:en:Minpon Shugi|Minpon Shugi]] is one form of democracy based on monarchical sovereignty that the political scientist Yoshino Sakuzo put forward in "{{nihongo||憲政の本義を説いて其有終の美を済すの途を論ず|Kensei no hongi o toite sono yushu no bi o nasu no michi o ronzu}}". According to this thesis, Minpon Shugi has two main points. First, it is "the policy in exercising political power of valuing the profit, happiness, and opinions of the people."<ref>{{Cite book|last=De Bary, Wm. Theodore, 1919-2017. Gluck, Carol, 1941- Tiedemann, Arthur E.|title=Sources of Japanese tradition.|date=2005|publisher=Columbia University Press|isbn=0-231-12984-X|pages=169|oclc=57750460}}</ref> Second, it demands that "in the final determination of policies, the people's opinions must be valued highly."<ref>{{Cite book|title=Sources of Japanese tradition|date=2001–2005|publisher=Columbia University Press|others=De Bary, Wm. Theodore, 1919-2017., Dykstra, Yoshiko Kurata.|isbn=0-231-12138-5|edition=2nd|location=New York|pages=172|oclc=45137685}}</ref> In short, Minpon Shugi does not fully specify where sovereignty lies, and it can coexist with the monarchical system.<ref>{{Cite book|last1=Mitani, Taichirō|title=Taishō demokurashī-ron : Yoshino Sakuzō no jidai|last2=三谷太一郎|date=15 August 2013|isbn=978-4-13-030157-2|edition=Daisanhan|location=Tōkyō|pages=146|oclc=856900297}}</ref>
At that time, people criticized Minshu Shugi, the concept of democracy based on popular sovereignty, because people were afraid of Minshu Shugi violating their monarchical system.<ref>{{Cite book|last1=Yoshino, Sakuzō, 1878-1933.|title=Yoshino Sakuzō|last2=吉野作造, 1878-1933|date=1984|publisher=Chūō Kōronsha|others=Mitani, Taichirō., 三谷太一郎.|isbn=4-12-400438-9|edition=Shohan|location=Tōkyō|pages=119|oclc=26618884}}</ref> Yoshino tried to change the concept of democracy based on people's sovereignty (Minshu Shugi), to a concept of democracy based on monarchical sovereignty (Minpon Shugi), in order to fit "democracy" into Japanese society.
After WWI, Minpon Shugi became the ideology of the Taishō Democracy Movement. The Taishō Democracy Movement was led by activists who were inspired by the Minpon Shugi that Yoshino advocated.<ref>{{Cite book|last1=Mitani, Taichirō|title=Taishō demokurashī-ron : Yoshino Sakuzō no jidai|last2=三谷太一郎|date=15 August 2013|isbn=978-4-13-030157-2|edition=Daisanhan|location=Tōkyō|pages=125|oclc=856900297}}</ref>
=== National discontent during the war === The Russo-Japanese War caused serious damage to Japan's finances. Japan used foreign debt to start the war. It is said that the reason why Japan barely won was that the Russian government held back its main force for fear of revolution. Japanese soldiers were plagued by Russian [[:en:machine gun|machine gun]]s and even some who survived were covered all over with wounds. The peace treaty offered by U.S. [[Theodore Roosevelt|President Roosevelt]] achieved "more than expected," said a government-leaning newspaper at the time.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Shinobu|first=Seizaburō|title=大正デモクラシー史|publisher=NIPPON HYORON SHA|year=1978|location=Tokyo|pages=14}}</ref>
On the other hand, the public reaction was the exact opposite. The people had sacrificed a great deal for the Russo-Japanese War. The government had imposed higher taxes on the citizens to pay for the war, and the peasants had had to pay most of their profits in taxes. The tax also dealt a blow to small and medium-sized businesses, and even during the war, the nation's silk cloth manufacturers' associations, wholesale brokers, union representatives, and the retail trade association came to Tokyo to oppose it. The same kind of opposition arose towards the salt monopoly.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Matsuo|first=Takayoshi|title=Taishō democracy|publisher=Iwanami Shoten|year=1994|isbn=978-4006000554|location=Tokyo|pages=29}}</ref> Thus, dissatisfaction with the war grew from rural areas across the country to urban areas.
== Effects of Taishō Democracy == The nation's trend towards universal democracy and independence can be seen as products of Taishō Democracy. These effects can be seen in various fields in that era, for example:
=== Politics === People started to express more of their opinions about the nation's decisions. In other words, people began to think for themselves, beyond what they were told by national leadership, and tried to exert political power to express their opinions. Political movements such as launching party and the movement for universal suffrage are some examples.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|last1=Mitani, Taichirō|title=Taishō demokurashī-ron : Yoshino Sakuzō no jidai|last2=三谷太一郎|date=15 August 2013|isbn=978-4-13-030157-2|edition=Daisanhan|location=Tōkyō|pages=1|oclc=856900297}}</ref>
=== Economy === The tendency of independence of the capital occurred and people sought to be free from the regulations. In other words, people demanded a [[:en:limited government|limited government]]. As a result, the power of capitalists or finance experts got stronger than before.<ref name=":1" /> Moreover, people began the movement of abolition of tax and claimed a disarmament.<ref>{{Cite book|last1=Mitani, Taichirō|title=Taishō demokurashī-ron : Yoshino Sakuzō no jidai|last2=三谷太一郎|date=15 August 2013|isbn=978-4-13-030157-2|edition=Daisanhan|location=Tōkyō|pages=12|oclc=856900297}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|last1=Matsuo, Takayoshi, 1929-2014.|title=Taishō demokurashī|last2=松尾尊兌, 1929-2014.|date=1994|publisher=Iwanami Shoten|isbn=4-00-260184-6|location=Tōkyō|pages=1|oclc=31151962}}</ref>
=== Education === Education for its own sake, independent of other concerns, was also advocated. University leadership and the press became independent of government, and people gained the ability to express their own opinions, independent from government views.<ref>{{Cite book|last1=Mitani, Taichirō|title=Taishō demokurashī-ron : Yoshino Sakuzō no jidai|last2=三谷太一郎|date=15 August 2013|isbn=978-4-13-030157-2|edition=Daisanhan|location=Tōkyō|pages=2|oclc=856900297}}</ref> Moreover, the increase in the literacy rate allowed people to read more books and get more information.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Nihon no kin-gendaishi o dō miru ka|date=2010|publisher=Iwanami Shoten|others=Iwanami Shoten. Iwanami Shinsho Henshūbu., 岩波書店. 岩波新書編集部.|isbn=978-4-00-431051-8|location=Tōkyō|pages=84|oclc=534643529}}</ref>
=== Women's Activists === The social system of patriarchy still existed in Japan at that time; however, there had been a gradual change to the traditional position of women. [[:en:Itō Noe|Itō Noe]], a feminist and editor of the magazine [[:en:Bluestocking (magazine)|''Seitō'']] ("Bluestocking"), complained that women did not have the same rights as men. Itō Noe, along with other women's activists such as [[:en:Hiratsuka Raichō|Hiratsuka Raichō]] and [[Nobuko Yoshiya|Yoshiya Nobuko]], tried to make changes to the system of [[:en:male privilege|male privilege]], and aimed for the independence of women.<ref>{{Cite book |title=日本の近現代史をどう見るか (Nihon no kin-gendaishi o dō miru ka; "How to view Japan's modern history") |date=2010 |publisher=岩波新書 (Iwanami Shinsho) |isbn=978-4-00-431051-8 |location=Tōkyō |pages=88–89 |oclc=534643529}}</ref>
== See also ==
* {{ill|Keishichi Hirasawa|ja|平澤計七|WD=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Keishichi Hirasawa|qq=}} (平澤計七) * [[:en:Yamamoto Senji|Senji Yamamoto]] (山本宣治) * [[Takiji Kobayashi]] (小林多喜二) * [[:en:Hiratsuka Raichō|Hiratsuka Raichō]] (平塚らいてう) * [[:en:Itō Noe|Itō Noe]] (伊藤野枝) * [[Nobuko Yoshiya|Yoshiya Nobuko]] (吉屋信子) * [[Women's suffrage in Japan|Woman's suffrage]] (女性参政権) * [[:en:Minpon Shugi|Minpon Shugi]] (民本主義) * [[Universal suffrage]] * [[:en:Freedom and People's Rights Movement|Freedom and People's Rights Movement]] (自由民権運動) * [[:en:Hanshinkan Modernism|Hanshinkan Modernism]]

revision.text:
{{Short description|Liberal trend in Japanese society (1904–1926)}}
'''Taishō Democracy''' was a liberal and democratic trend across the political, economic, and cultural fields in Japan that began roughly after the [[Russo-Japanese War]] and continued until the end of the [[Taishō era]] (1912{{endash}}1926). This trend was most evident in the field of politics, famously represented by the {{nihongo|Taishō Democracy Movement|大正デモクラシー運動}} and the establishment of the {{nihongo|Seitō Naikaku System|政党内閣制}}, a [[representative democracy]] in which the party with a majority in [[:en:National Diet|parliament]] organizes the cabinet.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|title=Nihon no kin-gendaishi o dō miru ka|date=2010|publisher=Iwanami Shoten|others=Iwanami Shoten. Iwanami Shinsho Henshūbu., 岩波書店. 岩波新書編集部.|isbn=978-4-00-431051-8|location=Tōkyō|pages=81|oclc=534643529}}</ref> The term "Taishō Democracy" has been widely used since the book "{{nihongo|The History of Taishō Democracy|大正デモクラシー史|Taishō demokurashii Shi}}" written by [[Shinobu Seizaburō]].<ref>{{Cite book|last=Shinobu|first=Seizaburo|title=Taisho Democrashii shi.|publisher=Nippon Hyoron Sha|year=1954|location=Tokyo}}</ref>
== Summary ==
=== Establishment === Although there are many theories about when Taishō Democracy began and ended, 1905 is often said to be the starting year of the Democratic era. 1905 was the year when [[:en:Bloody Sunday (1905)|Bloody Sunday]] happened in Russia, which subsequently led to the [[Russian Revolution]].<ref>{{Cite web|last=日本大百科全書（ニッポニカ）|title=ロシア革命|url=https://japanknowledge.com|access-date=2020-08-01|website=JapanKnowledge}}</ref> Bloody Sunday was instigated by loss of faith in the Tsarist government as a result of public discontent with the results of the Russo-Japanese War. In other words, the combination of the burden on the workers and general populace and the remote and costly war against Japan contributed greatly to the public unrest which sparked the 1905 revolution.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Shinobu|first=Seizaburo|title=Taisho Democrashii shi|publisher=Nippon Hyoron Sha|year=1954|location=Tokyo|pages=3}}</ref> Meanwhile, a similar combination existed in Japan. During the war and even before the war, Japanese socialists stated that the war would need sacrifice from the people. [[:en:Heimin Shinbun|Heimin Shinbun]] pointed out on 14 February 1904 that "The people will suffer for a long time from taxation for the war.".<ref>{{Cite book|last=Shinobu|first=Seizaburo|title=Taisho Democrashii shi|publisher=Nippon Hyoron Sha|year=1954|location=Tokyo|pages=7}}</ref> Some Christians and women's liberationists expressed similar anti-war attitudes.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Shinobu|first=Seizaburo|title=Taisho Democrashii shi|publisher=Nippon Hyoron Sha|year=1954|location=Tokyo|pages=9}}</ref>
The Russo-Japanese war ended in 1905, and [[Treaty of Portsmouth|the peace treaty]] was signed. Although Japan won, the general populace was disappointed by the result of the peace treaty.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Shinobu|first=Seizaburo|title=Taisho Democrashii shi|publisher=Nippon Hyoron Sha|year=1954|location=Tokyo|pages=15}}</ref> The Japanese people saw the peace treaty, which lacked major Russian territorial cessions and monetary reparations, as foreshadowing more sacrifice without benefits. Public anger toward the government gradually grew, and eventually led to the foundation of the movement against the Russo-Japanese war peace treaty.
=== Key Events === There are many theories about the timing of the Taishō Democracy, but many historians agree that the following events are crucial to Taishō Democracy.
First of all, there was the Russo-Japanese War peace treaty protest movement and the [[:en:Hibiya incendiary incident|Hibiya incendiary incident]] that accompanied it.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Shinobu|first=Seizaburo|title=Taisho Democrashii Shi|publisher=Nippon Hyoron Sya|year=1954|location=Tokyo|pages=39}}</ref> The movement grew across the country and gradually exposed the people's dissatisfaction with their government. Over time, the movement transformed into the Movement to Protect Constitutional Government, thereby igniting the {{nihongo|[[Taishō political crisis]]|大正政変}}.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Shinobu|first=Seizaburo|title=Taisho Democrashii Shi|publisher=Nippon Hyoron Sha|year=1954|location=Tokyo|pages=232}}</ref> This was the first instance of a popular movement trying to replace the cabinet in Japanese history, and the [[:en:Katsura Tarō|Katsura]] cabinet collapsed only 53 days after its formation.<ref>{{Cite web|last=日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ)|title=大正デモクラシー|url=https://japanknowledge.com|website=JapanKnowledge}}</ref> {{nihongo|[[Rice riots of 1918|The rice riots of 1918]]|米騒動}} and the establishment of the [[:en:Hara Takashi|Hara]] Cabinet are considered to be a major milestone in Taisho democracy.<ref name=":0" /> When the rice riots subsided, the {{nihongo|universal suffrage movement|普選運動}} was in full swing and became a nationwide political movement.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Shinobu|first=Seizaburo|title=Taisho Demokurashii Shi|publisher=Nippon Hyoron Sya|year=1954|location=Tokyo|pages=505}}</ref> The {{nihongo|[[:en:General Election Law|general election law]]|普通選挙法}} was enacted under the subsequent cabinet of {{nihongo|[[:en:Katō Takaaki|Takaaki Kato]]|加藤高明}}.<ref>{{Cite web|last=日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ)|title=普通選挙法|url=https://japanknowledge.com|website=JapanKnowledge}}</ref> In 1932, {{nihongo|[[Rikken Seiyūkai]]|政友会}}, {{nihongo|[[:en:Kenseikai|Kenseikai]]|憲政会}}, and {{nihongo|[[:en:Kakushin Club|Kakushin Club]]|革新倶楽部}} launched a campaign to overthrow [[:en:Kiyoura Keigo|Kiyoura Keigo]]'s cabinet. The campaign grew into what came to be called the {{nihongo|Second Constitutional Movement|第二次護憲運動}}.<ref>{{Cite web|last=国史大辞典|title=憲政擁護運動|url=https://japanknowledge.com|website=JapanKnowledge}}</ref>

According to this thesis, Minpon Shugi has two main points. First, it is "the policy in exercising political power of valuing the profit, happiness, and opinions of the people."<ref>{{Cite book|last=De Bary, Wm. Theodore, 1919-2017. Gluck, Carol, 1941- Tiedemann, Arthur E.|title=Sources of Japanese tradition.|date=2005|publisher=Columbia University Press|isbn=0-231-12984-X|pages=169|oclc=57750460}}</ref> Second, it demands that "in the final determination of policies, the people's opinions must be valued highly."<ref>{{Cite book|title=Sources of Japanese tradition|date=2001–2005|publisher=Columbia University Press|others=De Bary, Wm. Theodore, 1919-2017., Dykstra, Yoshiko Kurata.|isbn=0-231-12138-5|edition=2nd|location=New York|pages=172|oclc=45137685}}</ref> In short, Minpon Shugi does not fully specify where sovereignty lies, and it can coexist with the monarchical system.<ref>{{Cite book|last1=Mitani, Taichirō|title=Taishō demokurashī-ron : Yoshino Sakuzō no jidai|last2=三谷太一郎|date=15 August 2013|isbn=978-4-13-030157-2|edition=Daisanhan|location=Tōkyō|pages=146|oclc=856900297}}</ref>
At that time, people criticized Minshu Shugi, the concept of democracy based on popular sovereignty, because people were afraid of Minshu Shugi violating their monarchical system.<ref>{{Cite book|last1=Yoshino, Sakuzō, 1878-1933.|title=Yoshino Sakuzō|last2=吉野作造, 1878-1933|date=1984|publisher=Chūō Kōronsha|others=Mitani, Taichirō., 三谷太一郎.|isbn=4-12-400438-9|edition=Shohan|location=Tōkyō|pages=119|oclc=26618884}}</ref> Yoshino tried to change the concept of democracy based on people's sovereignty (Minshu Shugi), to a concept of democracy based on monarchical sovereignty (Minpon Shugi), in order to fit "democracy" into Japanese society.

The Taishō Democracy Movement was led by activists who were inspired by the Minpon Shugi that Yoshino advocated.<ref>{{Cite book|last1=Mitani, Taichirō|title=Taishō demokurashī-ron : Yoshino Sakuzō no jidai|last2=三谷太一郎|date=15 August 2013|isbn=978-4-13-030157-2|edition=Daisanhan|location=Tōkyō|pages=125|oclc=856900297}}</ref>
=== National discontent during the war === The Russo-Japanese War caused serious damage to Japan's finances. Japan used foreign debt to start the war. It is said that the reason why Japan barely won was that the Russian government held back its main force for fear of revolution. Japanese soldiers were plagued by Russian [[:en:machine gun|machine gun]]s and even some who survived were covered all over with wounds. The peace treaty offered by U.S. [[Theodore Roosevelt|President Roosevelt]] achieved "more than expected," said a government-leaning newspaper at the time.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Shinobu|first=Seizaburō|title=大正デモクラシー史|publisher=NIPPON HYORON SHA|year=1978|location=Tokyo|pages=14}}</ref>
On the other hand, the public reaction was the exact opposite. The people had sacrificed a great deal for the Russo-Japanese War. The government had imposed higher taxes on the citizens to pay for the war, and the peasants had had to pay most of their profits in taxes. The tax also dealt a blow to small and medium-sized businesses, and even during the war, the nation's silk cloth manufacturers' associations, wholesale brokers, union representatives, and the retail trade association came to Tokyo to oppose it. The same kind of opposition arose towards the salt monopoly.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Matsuo|first=Takayoshi|title=Taishō democracy|publisher=Iwanami Shoten|year=1994|isbn=978-4006000554|location=Tokyo|pages=29}}</ref> Thus, dissatisfaction with the war grew from rural areas across the country to urban areas.
== Effects of Taishō Democracy == The nation's trend towards universal democracy and independence can be seen as products of Taishō Democracy. These effects can be seen in various fields in that era, for example:
=== Politics === People started to express more of their opinions about the nation's decisions. In other words, people began to think for themselves, beyond what they were told by national leadership, and tried to exert political power to express their opinions. Political movements such as launching party and the movement for universal suffrage are some examples.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|last1=Mitani, Taichirō|title=Taishō demokurashī-ron : Yoshino Sakuzō no jidai|last2=三谷太一郎|date=15 August 2013|isbn=978-4-13-030157-2|edition=Daisanhan|location=Tōkyō|pages=1|oclc=856900297}}</ref>
=== Economy === The tendency of independence of the capital occurred and people sought to be free from the regulations. In other words, people demanded a [[:en:limited government|limited government]]. As a result, the power of capitalists or finance experts got stronger than before.<ref name=":1" /> Moreover, people began the movement of abolition of tax and claimed a disarmament.<ref>{{Cite book|last1=Mitani, Taichirō|title=Taishō demokurashī-ron : Yoshino Sakuzō no jidai|last2=三谷太一郎|date=15 August 2013|isbn=978-4-13-030157-2|edition=Daisanhan|location=Tōkyō|pages=12|oclc=856900297}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|last1=Matsuo, Takayoshi, 1929-2014.|title=Taishō demokurashī|last2=松尾尊兌, 1929-2014.|date=1994|publisher=Iwanami Shoten|isbn=4-00-260184-6|location=Tōkyō|pages=1|oclc=31151962}}</ref>
=== Education === Education for its own sake, independent of other concerns, was also advocated. University leadership and the press became independent of government, and people gained the ability to express their own opinions, independent from government views.<ref>{{Cite book|last1=Mitani, Taichirō|title=Taishō demokurashī-ron : Yoshino Sakuzō no jidai|last2=三谷太一郎|date=15 August 2013|isbn=978-4-13-030157-2|edition=Daisanhan|location=Tōkyō|pages=2|oclc=856900297}}</ref> Moreover, the increase in the literacy rate allowed people to read more books and get more information.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Nihon no kin-gendaishi o dō miru ka|date=2010|publisher=Iwanami Shoten|others=Iwanami Shoten. Iwanami Shinsho Henshūbu., 岩波書店. 岩波新書編集部.|isbn=978-4-00-431051-8|location=Tōkyō|pages=84|oclc=534643529}}</ref>
=== Women's Activists === The social system of patriarchy still existed in Japan at that time; however, there had been a gradual change to the traditional position of women. [[:en:Itō Noe|Itō Noe]], a feminist and editor of the magazine [[:en:Bluestocking (magazine)|''Seitō'']] ("Bluestocking"), complained that women did not have the same rights as men. Itō Noe, along with other women's activists such as [[:en:Hiratsuka Raichō|Hiratsuka Raichō]] and [[:en:Nobuko Yoshiya|Yoshiya Nobuko]], tried to make changes to the system of [[:en:male privilege|male privilege]], and aimed for the independence of women.<ref>{{Cite book |title=日本の近現代史をどう見るか (Nihon no kin-gendaishi o dō miru ka; "How to view Japan's modern history") |date=2010 |publisher=岩波新書 (Iwanami Shinsho) |isbn=978-4-00-431051-8 |location=Tōkyō |pages=88–89 |oclc=534643529}}</ref>
== See also ==
* {{ill|Keishichi Hirasawa|ja|平澤計七|WD=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Keishichi Hirasawa|qq=}} (平澤計七) * [[:en:Yamamoto Senji|Senji Yamamoto]] (山本宣治) * [[Takiji Kobayashi]] (小林多喜二) * [[:en:Hiratsuka Raichō|Hiratsuka Raichō]] (平塚らいてう) * [[:en:Itō Noe|Itō Noe]] (伊藤野枝) * [[:en:Nobuko Yoshiya|Yoshiya Nobuko]] (吉屋信子) * [[Women's suffrage in Japan|Woman's suffrage]] (女性参政権) * [[:en:Minpon Shugi|Minpon Shugi]] (民本主義) * [[Universal suffrage]] * [[:en:Freedom and People's Rights Movement|Freedom and People's Rights Movement]] (自由民権運動) * [[:en:Hanshinkan Modernism|Hanshinkan Modernism]]

revision.text:
{{Short description|Liberal trend in Japanese society (1904–1926)}}
'''Taishō Democracy''' was a liberal and democratic trend across the political, economic, and cultural fields in Japan that began roughly after the [[Russo-Japanese War]] and continued until the end of the [[:en:Taishō era|Taishō era]] (1912{{endash}}1926). This trend was most evident in the field of politics, famously represented by the {{nihongo|Taishō Democracy Movement|大正デモクラシー運動}} and the establishment of the {{nihongo|Seitō Naikaku System|政党内閣制}}, a [[representative democracy]] in which the party with a majority in [[:en:National Diet|parliament]] organizes the cabinet.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|title=Nihon no kin-gendaishi o dō miru ka|date=2010|publisher=Iwanami Shoten|others=Iwanami Shoten. Iwanami Shinsho Henshūbu., 岩波書店. 岩波新書編集部.|isbn=978-4-00-431051-8|location=Tōkyō|pages=81|oclc=534643529}}</ref> The term "Taishō Democracy" has been widely used since the book "{{nihongo|The History of Taishō Democracy|大正デモクラシー史|Taishō demokurashii Shi}}" written by [[Shinobu Seizaburō]].<ref>{{Cite book|last=Shinobu|first=Seizaburo|title=Taisho Democrashii shi.|publisher=Nippon Hyoron Sha|year=1954|location=Tokyo}}</ref>
== Summary ==
=== Establishment === Although there are many theories about when Taishō Democracy began and ended, 1905 is often said to be the starting year of the Democratic era. 1905 was the year when [[:en:Bloody Sunday (1905)|Bloody Sunday]] happened in Russia, which subsequently led to the [[Russian Revolution]].<ref>{{Cite web|last=日本大百科全書（ニッポニカ）|title=ロシア革命|url=https://japanknowledge.com|access-date=2020-08-01|website=JapanKnowledge}}</ref> Bloody Sunday was instigated by loss of faith in the Tsarist government as a result of public discontent with the results of the Russo-Japanese War. In other words, the combination of the burden on the workers and general populace and the remote and costly war against Japan contributed greatly to the public unrest which sparked the 1905 revolution.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Shinobu|first=Seizaburo|title=Taisho Democrashii shi|publisher=Nippon Hyoron Sha|year=1954|location=Tokyo|pages=3}}</ref> Meanwhile, a similar combination existed in Japan. During the war and even before the war, Japanese socialists stated that the war would need sacrifice from the people. [[:en:Heimin Shinbun|Heimin Shinbun]] pointed out on 14 February 1904 that "The people will suffer for a long time from taxation for the war.".<ref>{{Cite book|last=Shinobu|first=Seizaburo|title=Taisho Democrashii shi|publisher=Nippon Hyoron Sha|year=1954|location=Tokyo|pages=7}}</ref> Some Christians and women's liberationists expressed similar anti-war attitudes.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Shinobu|first=Seizaburo|title=Taisho Democrashii shi|publisher=Nippon Hyoron Sha|year=1954|location=Tokyo|pages=9}}</ref>
The Russo-Japanese war ended in 1905, and [[Treaty of Portsmouth|the peace treaty]] was signed. Although Japan won, the general populace was disappointed by the result of the peace treaty.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Shinobu|first=Seizaburo|title=Taisho Democrashii shi|publisher=Nippon Hyoron Sha|year=1954|location=Tokyo|pages=15}}</ref> The Japanese people saw the peace treaty, which lacked major Russian territorial cessions and monetary reparations, as foreshadowing more sacrifice without benefits. Public anger toward the government gradually grew, and eventually led to the foundation of the movement against the Russo-Japanese war peace treaty.
=== Key Events === There are many theories about the timing of the Taishō Democracy, but many historians agree that the following events are crucial to Taishō Democracy.
First of all, there was the Russo-Japanese War peace treaty protest movement and the [[:en:Hibiya incendiary incident|Hibiya incendiary incident]] that accompanied it.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Shinobu|first=Seizaburo|title=Taisho Democrashii Shi|publisher=Nippon Hyoron Sya|year=1954|location=Tokyo|pages=39}}</ref> The movement grew across the country and gradually exposed the people's dissatisfaction with their government. Over time, the movement transformed into the Movement to Protect Constitutional Government, thereby igniting the {{nihongo|[[Taishō political crisis]]|大正政変}}.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Shinobu|first=Seizaburo|title=Taisho Democrashii Shi|publisher=Nippon Hyoron Sha|year=1954|location=Tokyo|pages=232}}</ref> This was the first instance of a popular movement trying to replace the cabinet in Japanese history, and the [[:en:Katsura Tarō|Katsura]] cabinet collapsed only 53 days after its formation.<ref>{{Cite web|last=日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ)|title=大正デモクラシー|url=https://japanknowledge.com|website=JapanKnowledge}}</ref> {{nihongo|[[Rice riots of 1918|The rice riots of 1918]]|米騒動}} and the establishment of the [[:en:Hara Takashi|Hara]] Cabinet are considered to be a major milestone in Taisho democracy.<ref name=":0" /> When the rice riots subsided, the {{nihongo|universal suffrage movement|普選運動}} was in full swing and became a nationwide political movement.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Shinobu|first=Seizaburo|title=Taisho Demokurashii Shi|publisher=Nippon Hyoron Sya|year=1954|location=Tokyo|pages=505}}</ref> The {{nihongo|[[:en:General Election Law|general election law]]|普通選挙法}} was enacted under the subsequent cabinet of {{nihongo|[[:en:Katō Takaaki|Takaaki Kato]]|加藤高明}}.<ref>{{Cite web|last=日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ)|title=普通選挙法|url=https://japanknowledge.com|website=JapanKnowledge}}</ref> In 1932, {{nihongo|[[Rikken Seiyūkai]]|政友会}}, {{nihongo|[[:en:Kenseikai|Kenseikai]]|憲政会}}, and {{nihongo|[[:en:Kakushin Club|Kakushin Club]]|革新倶楽部}} launched a campaign to overthrow [[:en:Kiyoura Keigo|Kiyoura Keigo]]'s cabinet. The campaign grew into what came to be called the {{nihongo|Second Constitutional Movement|第二次護憲運動}}.<ref>{{Cite web|last=国史大辞典|title=憲政擁護運動|url=https://japanknowledge.com|website=JapanKnowledge}}</ref>
== Background ==
=== Minpon Shugi === [[:en:Minpon Shugi|Minpon Shugi]] is one form of democracy based on monarchical sovereignty that the political scientist Yoshino Sakuzo put forward in "{{nihongo||憲政の本義を説いて其有終の美を済すの途を論ず|Kensei no hongi o toite sono yushu no bi o nasu no michi o ronzu}}". According to this thesis, Minpon Shugi has two main points. First, it is "the policy in exercising political power of valuing the profit, happiness, and opinions of the people."<ref>{{Cite book|last=De Bary, Wm. Theodore, 1919-2017. Gluck, Carol, 1941- Tiedemann, Arthur E.|title=Sources of Japanese tradition.|date=2005|publisher=Columbia University Press|isbn=0-231-12984-X|pages=169|oclc=57750460}}</ref> Second, it demands that "in the final determination of policies, the people's opinions must be valued highly."<ref>{{Cite book|title=Sources of Japanese tradition|date=2001–2005|publisher=Columbia University Press|others=De Bary, Wm. Theodore, 1919-2017., Dykstra, Yoshiko Kurata.|isbn=0-231-12138-5|edition=2nd|location=New York|pages=172|oclc=45137685}}</ref> In short, Minpon Shugi does not fully specify where sovereignty lies, and it can coexist with the monarchical system.<ref>{{Cite book|last1=Mitani, Taichirō|title=Taishō demokurashī-ron : Yoshino Sakuzō no jidai|last2=三谷太一郎|date=15 August 2013|isbn=978-4-13-030157-2|edition=Daisanhan|location=Tōkyō|pages=146|oclc=856900297}}</ref>
At that time, people criticized Minshu Shugi, the concept of democracy based on popular sovereignty, because people were afraid of Minshu Shugi violating their monarchical system.<ref>{{Cite book|last1=Yoshino, Sakuzō, 1878-1933.|title=Yoshino Sakuzō|last2=吉野作造, 1878-1933|date=1984|publisher=Chūō Kōronsha|others=Mitani, Taichirō., 三谷太一郎.|isbn=4-12-400438-9|edition=Shohan|location=Tōkyō|pages=119|oclc=26618884}}</ref> Yoshino tried to change the concept of democracy based on people's sovereignty (Minshu Shugi), to a concept of democracy based on monarchical sovereignty (Minpon Shugi), in order to fit "democracy" into Japanese society.
After WWI, Minpon Shugi became the ideology of the Taishō Democracy Movement. The Taishō Democracy Movement was led by activists who were inspired by the Minpon Shugi that Yoshino advocated.<ref>{{Cite book|last1=Mitani, Taichirō|title=Taishō demokurashī-ron : Yoshino Sakuzō no jidai|last2=三谷太一郎|date=15 August 2013|isbn=978-4-13-030157-2|edition=Daisanhan|location=Tōkyō|pages=125|oclc=856900297}}</ref>
=== National discontent during the war === The Russo-Japanese War caused serious damage to Japan's finances. Japan used foreign debt to start the war. It is said that the reason why Japan barely won was that the Russian government held back its main force for fear of revolution. Japanese soldiers were plagued by Russian [[:en:machine gun|machine gun]]s and even some who survived were covered all over with wounds. The peace treaty offered by U.S. [[Theodore Roosevelt|President Roosevelt]] achieved "more than expected," said a government-leaning newspaper at the time.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Shinobu|first=Seizaburō|title=大正デモクラシー史|publisher=NIPPON HYORON SHA|year=1978|location=Tokyo|pages=14}}</ref>
On the other hand, the public reaction was the exact opposite. The people had sacrificed a great deal for the Russo-Japanese War. The government had imposed higher taxes on the citizens to pay for the war, and the peasants had had to pay most of their profits in taxes. The tax also dealt a blow to small and medium-sized businesses, and even during the war, the nation's silk cloth manufacturers' associations, wholesale brokers, union representatives, and the retail trade association came to Tokyo to oppose it. The same kind of opposition arose towards the salt monopoly.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Matsuo|first=Takayoshi|title=Taishō democracy|publisher=Iwanami Shoten|year=1994|isbn=978-4006000554|location=Tokyo|pages=29}}</ref> Thus, dissatisfaction with the war grew from rural areas across the country to urban areas.
== Effects of Taishō Democracy == The nation's trend towards universal democracy and independence can be seen as products of Taishō Democracy. These effects can be seen in various fields in that era, for example:
=== Politics === People started to express more of their opinions about the nation's decisions. In other words, people began to think for themselves, beyond what they were told by national leadership, and tried to exert political power to express their opinions. Political movements such as launching party and the movement for universal suffrage are some examples.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|last1=Mitani, Taichirō|title=Taishō demokurashī-ron : Yoshino Sakuzō no jidai|last2=三谷太一郎|date=15 August 2013|isbn=978-4-13-030157-2|edition=Daisanhan|location=Tōkyō|pages=1|oclc=856900297}}</ref>
=== Economy === The tendency of independence of the capital occurred and people sought to be free from the regulations. In other words, people demanded a [[:en:limited government|limited government]]. As a result, the power of capitalists or finance experts got stronger than before.<ref name=":1" /> Moreover, people began the movement of abolition of tax and claimed a disarmament.<ref>{{Cite book|last1=Mitani, Taichirō|title=Taishō demokurashī-ron : Yoshino Sakuzō no jidai|last2=三谷太一郎|date=15 August 2013|isbn=978-4-13-030157-2|edition=Daisanhan|location=Tōkyō|pages=12|oclc=856900297}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|last1=Matsuo, Takayoshi, 1929-2014.|title=Taishō demokurashī|last2=松尾尊兌, 1929-2014.|date=1994|publisher=Iwanami Shoten|isbn=4-00-260184-6|location=Tōkyō|pages=1|oclc=31151962}}</ref>
=== Education === Education for its own sake, independent of other concerns, was also advocated. University leadership and the press became independent of government, and people gained the ability to express their own opinions, independent from government views.<ref>{{Cite book|last1=Mitani, Taichirō|title=Taishō demokurashī-ron : Yoshino Sakuzō no jidai|last2=三谷太一郎|date=15 August 2013|isbn=978-4-13-030157-2|edition=Daisanhan|location=Tōkyō|pages=2|oclc=856900297}}</ref> Moreover, the increase in the literacy rate allowed people to read more books and get more information.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Nihon no kin-gendaishi o dō miru ka|date=2010|publisher=Iwanami Shoten|others=Iwanami Shoten. Iwanami Shinsho Henshūbu., 岩波書店. 岩波新書編集部.|isbn=978-4-00-431051-8|location=Tōkyō|pages=84|oclc=534643529}}</ref>
=== Women's Activists === The social system of patriarchy still existed in Japan at that time; however, there had been a gradual change to the traditional position of women. [[:en:Itō Noe|Itō Noe]], a feminist and editor of the magazine [[:en:Bluestocking (magazine)|''Seitō'']] ("Bluestocking"), complained that women did not have the same rights as men. Itō Noe, along with other women's activists such as [[:en:Hiratsuka Raichō|Hiratsuka Raichō]] and [[:en:Nobuko Yoshiya|Yoshiya Nobuko]], tried to make changes to the system of [[:en:male privilege|male privilege]], and aimed for the independence of women.<ref>{{Cite book |title=日本の近現代史をどう見るか (Nihon no kin-gendaishi o dō miru ka; "How to view Japan's modern history") |date=2010 |publisher=岩波新書 (Iwanami Shinsho) |isbn=978-4-00-431051-8 |location=Tōkyō |pages=88–89 |oclc=534643529}}</ref>
== See also ==
* {{ill|Keishichi Hirasawa|ja|平澤計七|WD=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Keishichi Hirasawa|qq=}} (平澤計七) * [[:en:Yamamoto Senji|Senji Yamamoto]] (山本宣治) * [[Takiji Kobayashi]] (小林多喜二) * [[:en:Hiratsuka Raichō|Hiratsuka Raichō]] (平塚らいてう) * [[:en:Itō Noe|Itō Noe]] (伊藤野枝) * [[:en:Nobuko Yoshiya|Yoshiya Nobuko]] (吉屋信子) * [[Women's suffrage in Japan|Woman's suffrage]] (女性参政権) * [[:en:Minpon Shugi|Minpon Shugi]] (民本主義) * [[Universal suffrage]] * [[:en:Freedom and People's Rights Movement|Freedom and People's Rights Movement]] (自由民権運動) * [[:en:Hanshinkan Modernism|Hanshinkan Modernism]]
== References == <references />
== External links == * https://www.facinghistory.org/nanjing-atrocities/nation-building/taisho-democracy-japan-1912-1926 * https://www.japantimes.co.jp/life/2012/07/29/general/taisho-democracy-pays-the-ultimate-price/#.XulryUX7Ryw

First of all, there was the Russo-Japanese War peace treaty protest movement and the [[:en:Hibiya incendiary incident|Hibiya incendiary incident]] that accompanied it.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Shinobu|first=Seizaburo|title=Taisho Democrashii Shi|publisher=Nippon Hyoron Sya|year=1954|location=Tokyo|pages=39}}</ref> The movement grew across the country and gradually exposed the people's dissatisfaction with their government. Over time, the movement transformed into the Movement to Protect Constitutional Government, thereby igniting the {{nihongo|[[:en:Taishō political crisis|Taishō political crisis]]|大正政変}}.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Shinobu|first=Seizaburo|title=Taisho Democrashii Shi|publisher=Nippon Hyoron Sha|year=1954|location=Tokyo|pages=232}}</ref> This was the first instance of a popular movement trying to replace the cabinet in Japanese history, and the [[:en:Katsura Tarō|Katsura]] cabinet collapsed only 53 days after its formation.<ref>{{Cite web|last=日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ)|title=大正デモクラシー|url=https://japanknowledge.com|website=JapanKnowledge}}</ref> {{nihongo|[[Rice riots of 1918|The rice riots of 1918]]|米騒動}} and the establishment of the [[:en:Hara Takashi|Hara]] Cabinet are considered to be a major milestone in Taisho democracy.<ref name=":0" /> When the rice riots subsided, the {{nihongo|universal suffrage movement|普選運動}} was in full swing and became a nationwide political movement.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Shinobu|first=Seizaburo|title=Taisho Demokurashii Shi|publisher=Nippon Hyoron Sya|year=1954|location=Tokyo|pages=505}}</ref> The {{nihongo|[[:en:General Election Law|general election law]]|普通選挙法}} was enacted under the subsequent cabinet of {{nihongo|[[:en:Katō Takaaki|Takaaki Kato]]|加藤高明}}.<ref>{{Cite web|last=日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ)|title=普通選挙法|url=https://japanknowledge.com|website=JapanKnowledge}}</ref> In 1932, {{nihongo|[[Rikken Seiyūkai]]|政友会}}, {{nihongo|[[:en:Kenseikai|Kenseikai]]|憲政会}}, and {{nihongo|[[:en:Kakushin Club|Kakushin Club]]|革新倶楽部}} launched a campaign to overthrow [[:en:Kiyoura Keigo|Kiyoura Keigo]]'s cabinet. The campaign grew into what came to be called the {{nihongo|Second Constitutional Movement|第二次護憲運動}}.<ref>{{Cite web|last=国史大辞典|title=憲政擁護運動|url=https://japanknowledge.com|website=JapanKnowledge}}</ref>
== Background ==
=== Minpon Shugi === [[:en:Minpon Shugi|Minpon Shugi]] is one form of democracy based on monarchical sovereignty that the political scientist Yoshino Sakuzo put forward in "{{nihongo||憲政の本義を説いて其有終の美を済すの途を論ず|Kensei no hongi o toite sono yushu no bi o nasu no michi o ronzu}}". According to this thesis, Minpon Shugi has two main points. First, it is "the policy in exercising political power of valuing the profit, happiness, and opinions of the people."<ref>{{Cite book|last=De Bary, Wm. Theodore, 1919-2017. Gluck, Carol, 1941- Tiedemann, Arthur E.|title=Sources of Japanese tradition.|date=2005|publisher=Columbia University Press|isbn=0-231-12984-X|pages=169|oclc=57750460}}</ref> Second, it demands that "in the final determination of policies, the people's opinions must be valued highly."<ref>{{Cite book|title=Sources of Japanese tradition|date=2001–2005|publisher=Columbia University Press|others=De Bary, Wm. Theodore, 1919-2017., Dykstra, Yoshiko Kurata.|isbn=0-231-12138-5|edition=2nd|location=New York|pages=172|oclc=45137685}}</ref> In short, Minpon Shugi does not fully specify where sovereignty lies, and it can coexist with the monarchical system.<ref>{{Cite book|last1=Mitani, Taichirō|title=Taishō demokurashī-ron : Yoshino Sakuzō no jidai|last2=三谷太一郎|date=15 August 2013|isbn=978-4-13-030157-2|edition=Daisanhan|location=Tōkyō|pages=146|oclc=856900297}}</ref>
At that time, people criticized Minshu Shugi, the concept of democracy based on popular sovereignty, because people were afraid of Minshu Shugi violating their monarchical system.<ref>{{Cite book|last1=Yoshino, Sakuzō, 1878-1933.|title=Yoshino Sakuzō|last2=吉野作造, 1878-1933|date=1984|publisher=Chūō Kōronsha|others=Mitani, Taichirō., 三谷太一郎.|isbn=4-12-400438-9|edition=Shohan|location=Tōkyō|pages=119|oclc=26618884}}</ref> Yoshino tried to change the concept of democracy based on people's sovereignty (Minshu Shugi), to a concept of democracy based on monarchical sovereignty (Minpon Shugi), in order to fit "democracy" into Japanese society.
After WWI, Minpon Shugi became the ideology of the Taishō Democracy Movement. The Taishō Democracy Movement was led by activists who were inspired by the Minpon Shugi that Yoshino advocated.<ref>{{Cite book|last1=Mitani, Taichirō|title=Taishō demokurashī-ron : Yoshino Sakuzō no jidai|last2=三谷太一郎|date=15 August 2013|isbn=978-4-13-030157-2|edition=Daisanhan|location=Tōkyō|pages=125|oclc=856900297}}</ref>
=== National discontent during the war === The Russo-Japanese War caused serious damage to Japan's finances. Japan used foreign debt to start the war. It is said that the reason why Japan barely won was that the Russian government held back its main force for fear of revolution. Japanese soldiers were plagued by Russian [[:en:machine gun|machine gun]]s and even some who survived were covered all over with wounds. The peace treaty offered by U.S. [[Theodore Roosevelt|President Roosevelt]] achieved "more than expected," said a government-leaning newspaper at the time.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Shinobu|first=Seizaburō|title=大正デモクラシー史|publisher=NIPPON HYORON SHA|year=1978|location=Tokyo|pages=14}}</ref>
On the other hand, the public reaction was the exact opposite. The people had sacrificed a great deal for the Russo-Japanese War. The government had imposed higher taxes on the citizens to pay for the war, and the peasants had had to pay most of their profits in taxes. The tax also dealt a blow to small and medium-sized businesses, and even during the war, the nation's silk cloth manufacturers' associations, wholesale brokers, union representatives, and the retail trade association came to Tokyo to oppose it. The same kind of opposition arose towards the salt monopoly.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Matsuo|first=Takayoshi|title=Taishō democracy|publisher=Iwanami Shoten|year=1994|isbn=978-4006000554|location=Tokyo|pages=29}}</ref> Thus, dissatisfaction with the war grew from rural areas across the country to urban areas.
== Effects of Taishō Democracy == The nation's trend towards universal democracy and independence can be seen as products of Taishō Democracy. These effects can be seen in various fields in that era, for example:
=== Politics === People started to express more of their opinions about the nation's decisions. In other words, people began to think for themselves, beyond what they were told by national leadership, and tried to exert political power to express their opinions. Political movements such as launching party and the movement for universal suffrage are some examples.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|last1=Mitani, Taichirō|title=Taishō demokurashī-ron : Yoshino Sakuzō no jidai|last2=三谷太一郎|date=15 August 2013|isbn=978-4-13-030157-2|edition=Daisanhan|location=Tōkyō|pages=1|oclc=856900297}}</ref>
=== Economy === The tendency of independence of the capital occurred and people sought to be free from the regulations. In other words, people demanded a [[:en:limited government|limited government]]. As a result, the power of capitalists or finance experts got stronger than before.<ref name=":1" /> Moreover, people began the movement of abolition of tax and claimed a disarmament.<ref>{{Cite book|last1=Mitani, Taichirō|title=Taishō demokurashī-ron : Yoshino Sakuzō no jidai|last2=三谷太一郎|date=15 August 2013|isbn=978-4-13-030157-2|edition=Daisanhan|location=Tōkyō|pages=12|oclc=856900297}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|last1=Matsuo, Takayoshi, 1929-2014.|title=Taishō demokurashī|last2=松尾尊兌, 1929-2014.|date=1994|publisher=Iwanami Shoten|isbn=4-00-260184-6|location=Tōkyō|pages=1|oclc=31151962}}</ref>
=== Education === Education for its own sake, independent of other concerns, was also advocated. University leadership and the press became independent of government, and people gained the ability to express their own opinions, independent from government views.<ref>{{Cite book|last1=Mitani, Taichirō|title=Taishō demokurashī-ron : Yoshino Sakuzō no jidai|last2=三谷太一郎|date=15 August 2013|isbn=978-4-13-030157-2|edition=Daisanhan|location=Tōkyō|pages=2|oclc=856900297}}</ref> Moreover, the increase in the literacy rate allowed people to read more books and get more information.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Nihon no kin-gendaishi o dō miru ka|date=2010|publisher=Iwanami Shoten|others=Iwanami Shoten. Iwanami Shinsho Henshūbu., 岩波書店. 岩波新書編集部.|isbn=978-4-00-431051-8|location=Tōkyō|pages=84|oclc=534643529}}</ref>
=== Women's Activists === The social system of patriarchy still existed in Japan at that time; however, there had been a gradual change to the traditional position of women. [[:en:Itō Noe|Itō Noe]], a feminist and editor of the magazine [[:en:Bluestocking (magazine)|''Seitō'']] ("Bluestocking"), complained that women did not have the same rights as men. Itō Noe, along with other women's activists such as [[:en:Hiratsuka Raichō|Hiratsuka Raichō]] and [[:en:Nobuko Yoshiya|Yoshiya Nobuko]], tried to make changes to the system of [[:en:male privilege|male privilege]], and aimed for the independence of women.<ref>{{Cite book |title=日本の近現代史をどう見るか (Nihon no kin-gendaishi o dō miru ka; "How to view Japan's modern history") |date=2010 |publisher=岩波新書 (Iwanami Shinsho) |isbn=978-4-00-431051-8 |location=Tōkyō |pages=88–89 |oclc=534643529}}</ref>
== See also ==

revision.text:
{{Short description|Liberal trend in Japanese society (1904–1926)}}
'''Taishō Democracy''' was a liberal and democratic trend across the political, economic, and cultural fields in Japan that began roughly after the [[Russo-Japanese War]] and continued until the end of the [[:en:Taishō era|Taishō era]] (1912{{endash}}1926). This trend was most evident in the field of politics, famously represented by the {{nihongo|Taishō Democracy Movement|大正デモクラシー運動}} and the establishment of the {{nihongo|Seitō Naikaku System|政党内閣制}}, a [[:en:Representative democracy|Representative democracy]] in which the party with a majority in [[:en:National Diet|parliament]] organizes the cabinet.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|title=Nihon no kin-gendaishi o dō miru ka|date=2010|publisher=Iwanami Shoten|others=Iwanami Shoten. Iwanami Shinsho Henshūbu., 岩波書店. 岩波新書編集部.|isbn=978-4-00-431051-8|location=Tōkyō|pages=81|oclc=534643529}}</ref> The term "Taishō Democracy" has been widely used since the book "{{nihongo|The History of Taishō Democracy|大正デモクラシー史|Taishō demokurashii Shi}}" written by [[Shinobu Seizaburō]].<ref>{{Cite book|last=Shinobu|first=Seizaburo|title=Taisho Democrashii shi.|publisher=Nippon Hyoron Sha|year=1954|location=Tokyo}}</ref>
== Summary ==
=== Establishment === Although there are many theories about when Taishō Democracy began and ended, 1905 is often said to be the starting year of the Democratic era. 1905 was the year when [[:en:Bloody Sunday (1905)|Bloody Sunday]] happened in Russia, which subsequently led to the [[Russian Revolution]].<ref>{{Cite web|last=日本大百科全書（ニッポニカ）|title=ロシア革命|url=https://japanknowledge.com|access-date=2020-08-01|website=JapanKnowledge}}</ref> Bloody Sunday was instigated by loss of faith in the Tsarist government as a result of public discontent with the results of the Russo-Japanese War. In other words, the combination of the burden on the workers and general populace and the remote and costly war against Japan contributed greatly to the public unrest which sparked the 1905 revolution.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Shinobu|first=Seizaburo|title=Taisho Democrashii shi|publisher=Nippon Hyoron Sha|year=1954|location=Tokyo|pages=3}}</ref> Meanwhile, a similar combination existed in Japan. During the war and even before the war, Japanese socialists stated that the war would need sacrifice from the people. [[:en:Heimin Shinbun|Heimin Shinbun]] pointed out on 14 February 1904 that "The people will suffer for a long time from taxation for the war.".<ref>{{Cite book|last=Shinobu|first=Seizaburo|title=Taisho Democrashii shi|publisher=Nippon Hyoron Sha|year=1954|location=Tokyo|pages=7}}</ref> Some Christians and women's liberationists expressed similar anti-war attitudes.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Shinobu|first=Seizaburo|title=Taisho Democrashii shi|publisher=Nippon Hyoron Sha|year=1954|location=Tokyo|pages=9}}</ref>
The Russo-Japanese war ended in 1905, and [[Treaty of Portsmouth|the peace treaty]] was signed. Although Japan won, the general populace was disappointed by the result of the peace treaty.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Shinobu|first=Seizaburo|title=Taisho Democrashii shi|publisher=Nippon Hyoron Sha|year=1954|location=Tokyo|pages=15}}</ref> The Japanese people saw the peace treaty, which lacked major Russian territorial cessions and monetary reparations, as foreshadowing more sacrifice without benefits. Public anger toward the government gradually grew, and eventually led to the foundation of the movement against the Russo-Japanese war peace treaty.
=== Key Events === There are many theories about the timing of the Taishō Democracy, but many historians agree that the following events are crucial to Taishō Democracy.
First of all, there was the Russo-Japanese War peace treaty protest movement and the [[:en:Hibiya incendiary incident|Hibiya incendiary incident]] that accompanied it.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Shinobu|first=Seizaburo|title=Taisho Democrashii Shi|publisher=Nippon Hyoron Sya|year=1954|location=Tokyo|pages=39}}</ref> The movement grew across the country and gradually exposed the people's dissatisfaction with their government. Over time, the movement transformed into the Movement to Protect Constitutional Government, thereby igniting the {{nihongo|[[:en:Taishō political crisis|Taishō political crisis]]|大正政変}}.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Shinobu|first=Seizaburo|title=Taisho Democrashii Shi|publisher=Nippon Hyoron Sha|year=1954|location=Tokyo|pages=232}}</ref> This was the first instance of a popular movement trying to replace the cabinet in Japanese history, and the [[:en:Katsura Tarō|Katsura]] cabinet collapsed only 53 days after its formation.<ref>{{Cite web|last=日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ)|title=大正デモクラシー|url=https://japanknowledge.com|website=JapanKnowledge}}</ref> {{nihongo|[[Rice riots of 1918|The rice riots of 1918]]|米騒動}} and the establishment of the [[:en:Hara Takashi|Hara]] Cabinet are considered to be a major milestone in Taisho democracy.<ref name=":0" /> When the rice riots subsided, the {{nihongo|universal suffrage movement|普選運動}} was in full swing and became a nationwide political movement.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Shinobu|first=Seizaburo|title=Taisho Demokurashii Shi|publisher=Nippon Hyoron Sya|year=1954|location=Tokyo|pages=505}}</ref> The {{nihongo|[[:en:General Election Law|general election law]]|普通選挙法}} was enacted under the subsequent cabinet of {{nihongo|[[:en:Katō Takaaki|Takaaki Kato]]|加藤高明}}.<ref>{{Cite web|last=日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ)|title=普通選挙法|url=https://japanknowledge.com|website=JapanKnowledge}}</ref> In 1932, {{nihongo|[[Rikken Seiyūkai]]|政友会}}, {{nihongo|[[:en:Kenseikai|Kenseikai]]|憲政会}}, and {{nihongo|[[:en:Kakushin Club|Kakushin Club]]|革新倶楽部}} launched a campaign to overthrow [[:en:Kiyoura Keigo|Kiyoura Keigo]]'s cabinet. The campaign grew into what came to be called the {{nihongo|Second Constitutional Movement|第二次護憲運動}}.<ref>{{Cite web|last=国史大辞典|title=憲政擁護運動|url=https://japanknowledge.com|website=JapanKnowledge}}</ref>
== Background ==
=== Minpon Shugi === [[:en:Minpon Shugi|Minpon Shugi]] is one form of democracy based on monarchical sovereignty that the political scientist Yoshino Sakuzo put forward in "{{nihongo||憲政の本義を説いて其有終の美を済すの途を論ず|Kensei no hongi o toite sono yushu no bi o nasu no michi o ronzu}}". According to this thesis, Minpon Shugi has two main points. First, it is "the policy in exercising political power of valuing the profit, happiness, and opinions of the people."<ref>{{Cite book|last=De Bary, Wm. Theodore, 1919-2017. Gluck, Carol, 1941- Tiedemann, Arthur E.|title=Sources of Japanese tradition.|date=2005|publisher=Columbia University Press|isbn=0-231-12984-X|pages=169|oclc=57750460}}</ref> Second, it demands that "in the final determination of policies, the people's opinions must be valued highly."<ref>{{Cite book|title=Sources of Japanese tradition|date=2001–2005|publisher=Columbia University Press|others=De Bary, Wm. Theodore, 1919-2017., Dykstra, Yoshiko Kurata.|isbn=0-231-12138-5|edition=2nd|location=New York|pages=172|oclc=45137685}}</ref> In short, Minpon Shugi does not fully specify where sovereignty lies, and it can coexist with the monarchical system.<ref>{{Cite book|last1=Mitani, Taichirō|title=Taishō demokurashī-ron : Yoshino Sakuzō no jidai|last2=三谷太一郎|date=15 August 2013|isbn=978-4-13-030157-2|edition=Daisanhan|location=Tōkyō|pages=146|oclc=856900297}}</ref>
At that time, people criticized Minshu Shugi, the concept of democracy based on popular sovereignty, because people were afraid of Minshu Shugi violating their monarchical system.<ref>{{Cite book|last1=Yoshino, Sakuzō, 1878-1933.|title=Yoshino Sakuzō|last2=吉野作造, 1878-1933|date=1984|publisher=Chūō Kōronsha|others=Mitani, Taichirō., 三谷太一郎.|isbn=4-12-400438-9|edition=Shohan|location=Tōkyō|pages=119|oclc=26618884}}</ref> Yoshino tried to change the concept of democracy based on people's sovereignty (Minshu Shugi), to a concept of democracy based on monarchical sovereignty (Minpon Shugi), in order to fit "democracy" into Japanese society.
After WWI, Minpon Shugi became the ideology of the Taishō Democracy Movement. The Taishō Democracy Movement was led by activists who were inspired by the Minpon Shugi that Yoshino advocated.<ref>{{Cite book|last1=Mitani, Taichirō|title=Taishō demokurashī-ron : Yoshino Sakuzō no jidai|last2=三谷太一郎|date=15 August 2013|isbn=978-4-13-030157-2|edition=Daisanhan|location=Tōkyō|pages=125|oclc=856900297}}</ref>
=== National discontent during the war === The Russo-Japanese War caused serious damage to Japan's finances. Japan used foreign debt to start the war. It is said that the reason why Japan barely won was that the Russian government held back its main force for fear of revolution. Japanese soldiers were plagued by Russian [[:en:machine gun|machine gun]]s and even some who survived were covered all over with wounds. The peace treaty offered by U.S. [[Theodore Roosevelt|President Roosevelt]] achieved "more than expected," said a government-leaning newspaper at the time.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Shinobu|first=Seizaburō|title=大正デモクラシー史|publisher=NIPPON HYORON SHA|year=1978|location=Tokyo|pages=14}}</ref>
On the other hand, the public reaction was the exact opposite. The people had sacrificed a great deal for the Russo-Japanese War. The government had imposed higher taxes on the citizens to pay for the war, and the peasants had had to pay most of their profits in taxes. The tax also dealt a blow to small and medium-sized businesses, and even during the war, the nation's silk cloth manufacturers' associations, wholesale brokers, union representatives, and the retail trade association came to Tokyo to oppose it. The same kind of opposition arose towards the salt monopoly.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Matsuo|first=Takayoshi|title=Taishō democracy|publisher=Iwanami Shoten|year=1994|isbn=978-4006000554|location=Tokyo|pages=29}}</ref> Thus, dissatisfaction with the war grew from rural areas across the country to urban areas.
== Effects of Taishō Democracy == The nation's trend towards universal democracy and independence can be seen as products of Taishō Democracy. These effects can be seen in various fields in that era, for example:
=== Politics === People started to express more of their opinions about the nation's decisions. In other words, people began to think for themselves, beyond what they were told by national leadership, and tried to exert political power to express their opinions. Political movements such as launching party and the movement for universal suffrage are some examples.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|last1=Mitani, Taichirō|title=Taishō demokurashī-ron : Yoshino Sakuzō no jidai|last2=三谷太一郎|date=15 August 2013|isbn=978-4-13-030157-2|edition=Daisanhan|location=Tōkyō|pages=1|oclc=856900297}}</ref>
=== Economy === The tendency of independence of the capital occurred and people sought to be free from the regulations. In other words, people demanded a [[:en:limited government|limited government]]. As a result, the power of capitalists or finance experts got stronger than before.<ref name=":1" /> Moreover, people began the movement of abolition of tax and claimed a disarmament.<ref>{{Cite book|last1=Mitani, Taichirō|title=Taishō demokurashī-ron : Yoshino Sakuzō no jidai|last2=三谷太一郎|date=15 August 2013|isbn=978-4-13-030157-2|edition=Daisanhan|location=Tōkyō|pages=12|oclc=856900297}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|last1=Matsuo, Takayoshi, 1929-2014.|title=Taishō demokurashī|last2=松尾尊兌, 1929-2014.|date=1994|publisher=Iwanami Shoten|isbn=4-00-260184-6|location=Tōkyō|pages=1|oclc=31151962}}</ref>
=== Education === Education for its own sake, independent of other concerns, was also advocated. University leadership and the press became independent of government, and people gained the ability to express their own opinions, independent from government views.<ref>{{Cite book|last1=Mitani, Taichirō|title=Taishō demokurashī-ron : Yoshino Sakuzō no jidai|last2=三谷太一郎|date=15 August 2013|isbn=978-4-13-030157-2|edition=Daisanhan|location=Tōkyō|pages=2|oclc=856900297}}</ref> Moreover, the increase in the literacy rate allowed people to read more books and get more information.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Nihon no kin-gendaishi o dō miru ka|date=2010|publisher=Iwanami Shoten|others=Iwanami Shoten. Iwanami Shinsho Henshūbu., 岩波書店. 岩波新書編集部.|isbn=978-4-00-431051-8|location=Tōkyō|pages=84|oclc=534643529}}</ref>
=== Women's Activists === The social system of patriarchy still existed in Japan at that time; however, there had been a gradual change to the traditional position of women. [[:en:Itō Noe|Itō Noe]], a feminist and editor of the magazine [[:en:Bluestocking (magazine)|''Seitō'']] ("Bluestocking"), complained that women did not have the same rights as men. Itō Noe, along with other women's activists such as [[:en:Hiratsuka Raichō|Hiratsuka Raichō]] and [[:en:Nobuko Yoshiya|Yoshiya Nobuko]], tried to make changes to the system of [[:en:male privilege|male privilege]], and aimed for the independence of women.<ref>{{Cite book |title=日本の近現代史をどう見るか (Nihon no kin-gendaishi o dō miru ka; "How to view Japan's modern history") |date=2010 |publisher=岩波新書 (Iwanami Shinsho) |isbn=978-4-00-431051-8 |location=Tōkyō |pages=88–89 |oclc=534643529}}</ref>
== See also ==

revision.text:
{{Short description|Liberal trend in Japanese society (1904–1926)}}
'''Taishō Democracy''' was a liberal and democratic trend across the political, economic, and cultural fields in Japan that began roughly after the [[Russo-Japanese War]] and continued until the end of the [[:en:Taishō era|Taishō era]] (1912{{endash}}1926). This trend was most evident in the field of politics, famously represented by the {{nihongo|Taishō Democracy Movement|大正デモクラシー運動}} and the establishment of the {{nihongo|Seitō Naikaku System|政党内閣制}}, a [[:en:Representative democracy|Representative democracy]] in which the party with a majority in [[:en:National Diet|parliament]] organizes the cabinet.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|title=Nihon no kin-gendaishi o dō miru ka|date=2010|publisher=Iwanami Shoten|others=Iwanami Shoten. Iwanami Shinsho Henshūbu., 岩波書店. 岩波新書編集部.|isbn=978-4-00-431051-8|location=Tōkyō|pages=81|oclc=534643529}}</ref> The term "Taishō Democracy" has been widely used since the book "{{nihongo|The History of Taishō Democracy|大正デモクラシー史|Taishō demokurashii Shi}}" written by [[Shinobu Seizaburō]].<ref>{{Cite book|last=Shinobu|first=Seizaburo|title=Taisho Democrashii shi.|publisher=Nippon Hyoron Sha|year=1954|location=Tokyo}}</ref>
== Summary ==
=== Establishment === Although there are many theories about when Taishō Democracy began and ended, 1905 is often said to be the starting year of the Democratic era. 1905 was the year when [[:en:Bloody Sunday (1905)|Bloody Sunday]] happened in Russia, which subsequently led to the [[Russian Revolution]].<ref>{{Cite web|last=日本大百科全書（ニッポニカ）|title=ロシア革命|url=https://japanknowledge.com|access-date=2020-08-01|website=JapanKnowledge}}</ref> Bloody Sunday was instigated by loss of faith in the Tsarist government as a result of public discontent with the results of the Russo-Japanese War. In other words, the combination of the burden on the workers and general populace and the remote and costly war against Japan contributed greatly to the public unrest which sparked the 1905 revolution.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Shinobu|first=Seizaburo|title=Taisho Democrashii shi|publisher=Nippon Hyoron Sha|year=1954|location=Tokyo|pages=3}}</ref> Meanwhile, a similar combination existed in Japan. During the war and even before the war, Japanese socialists stated that the war would need sacrifice from the people. [[:en:Heimin Shinbun|Heimin Shinbun]] pointed out on 14 February 1904 that "The people will suffer for a long time from taxation for the war.".<ref>{{Cite book|last=Shinobu|first=Seizaburo|title=Taisho Democrashii shi|publisher=Nippon Hyoron Sha|year=1954|location=Tokyo|pages=7}}</ref> Some Christians and women's liberationists expressed similar anti-war attitudes.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Shinobu|first=Seizaburo|title=Taisho Democrashii shi|publisher=Nippon Hyoron Sha|year=1954|location=Tokyo|pages=9}}</ref>
The Russo-Japanese war ended in 1905, and [[Treaty of Portsmouth|the peace treaty]] was signed. Although Japan won, the general populace was disappointed by the result of the peace treaty.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Shinobu|first=Seizaburo|title=Taisho Democrashii shi|publisher=Nippon Hyoron Sha|year=1954|location=Tokyo|pages=15}}</ref> The Japanese people saw the peace treaty, which lacked major Russian territorial cessions and monetary reparations, as foreshadowing more sacrifice without benefits. Public anger toward the government gradually grew, and eventually led to the foundation of the movement against the Russo-Japanese war peace treaty.
=== Key Events === There are many theories about the timing of the Taishō Democracy, but many historians agree that the following events are crucial to Taishō Democracy.
First of all, there was the Russo-Japanese War peace treaty protest movement and the [[:en:Hibiya incendiary incident|Hibiya incendiary incident]] that accompanied it.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Shinobu|first=Seizaburo|title=Taisho Democrashii Shi|publisher=Nippon Hyoron Sya|year=1954|location=Tokyo|pages=39}}</ref> The movement grew across the country and gradually exposed the people's dissatisfaction with their government. Over time, the movement transformed into the Movement to Protect Constitutional Government, thereby igniting the {{nihongo|[[:en:Taishō political crisis|Taishō political crisis]]|大正政変}}.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Shinobu|first=Seizaburo|title=Taisho Democrashii Shi|publisher=Nippon Hyoron Sha|year=1954|location=Tokyo|pages=232}}</ref> This was the first instance of a popular movement trying to replace the cabinet in Japanese history, and the [[:en:Katsura Tarō|Katsura]] cabinet collapsed only 53 days after its formation.<ref>{{Cite web|last=日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ)|title=大正デモクラシー|url=https://japanknowledge.com|website=JapanKnowledge}}</ref> {{nihongo|[[Rice riots of 1918|The rice riots of 1918]]|米騒動}} and the establishment of the [[:en:Hara Takashi|Hara]] Cabinet are considered to be a major milestone in Taisho democracy.<ref name=":0" /> When the rice riots subsided, the {{nihongo|universal suffrage movement|普選運動}} was in full swing and became a nationwide political movement.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Shinobu|first=Seizaburo|title=Taisho Demokurashii Shi|publisher=Nippon Hyoron Sya|year=1954|location=Tokyo|pages=505}}</ref> The {{nihongo|[[:en:General Election Law|general election law]]|普通選挙法}} was enacted under the subsequent cabinet of {{nihongo|[[:en:Katō Takaaki|Takaaki Kato]]|加藤高明}}.<ref>{{Cite web|last=日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ)|title=普通選挙法|url=https://japanknowledge.com|website=JapanKnowledge}}</ref> In 1932, {{nihongo|[[Rikken Seiyūkai]]|政友会}}, {{nihongo|[[:en:Kenseikai|Kenseikai]]|憲政会}}, and {{nihongo|[[:en:Kakushin Club|Kakushin Club]]|革新倶楽部}} launched a campaign to overthrow [[:en:Kiyoura Keigo|Kiyoura Keigo]]'s cabinet. The campaign grew into what came to be called the {{nihongo|Second Constitutional Movement|第二次護憲運動}}.<ref>{{Cite web|last=国史大辞典|title=憲政擁護運動|url=https://japanknowledge.com|website=JapanKnowledge}}</ref>
== Background ==
=== Minpon Shugi === [[:en:Minpon Shugi|Minpon Shugi]] is one form of democracy based on monarchical sovereignty that the political scientist Yoshino Sakuzo put forward in "{{nihongo||憲政の本義を説いて其有終の美を済すの途を論ず|Kensei no hongi o toite sono yushu no bi o nasu no michi o ronzu}}". According to this thesis, Minpon Shugi has two main points. First, it is "the policy in exercising political power of valuing the profit, happiness, and opinions of the people."<ref>{{Cite book|last=De Bary, Wm. Theodore, 1919-2017. Gluck, Carol, 1941- Tiedemann, Arthur E.|title=Sources of Japanese tradition.|date=2005|publisher=Columbia University Press|isbn=0-231-12984-X|pages=169|oclc=57750460}}</ref> Second, it demands that "in the final determination of policies, the people's opinions must be valued highly."<ref>{{Cite book|title=Sources of Japanese tradition|date=2001–2005|publisher=Columbia University Press|others=De Bary, Wm. Theodore, 1919-2017., Dykstra, Yoshiko Kurata.|isbn=0-231-12138-5|edition=2nd|location=New York|pages=172|oclc=45137685}}</ref> In short, Minpon Shugi does not fully specify where sovereignty lies, and it can coexist with the monarchical system.<ref>{{Cite book|last1=Mitani, Taichirō|title=Taishō demokurashī-ron : Yoshino Sakuzō no jidai|last2=三谷太一郎|date=15 August 2013|isbn=978-4-13-030157-2|edition=Daisanhan|location=Tōkyō|pages=146|oclc=856900297}}</ref>
At that time, people criticized Minshu Shugi, the concept of democracy based on popular sovereignty, because people were afraid of Minshu Shugi violating their monarchical system.<ref>{{Cite book|last1=Yoshino, Sakuzō, 1878-1933.|title=Yoshino Sakuzō|last2=吉野作造, 1878-1933|date=1984|publisher=Chūō Kōronsha|others=Mitani, Taichirō., 三谷太一郎.|isbn=4-12-400438-9|edition=Shohan|location=Tōkyō|pages=119|oclc=26618884}}</ref> Yoshino tried to change the concept of democracy based on people's sovereignty (Minshu Shugi), to a concept of democracy based on monarchical sovereignty (Minpon Shugi), in order to fit "democracy" into Japanese society.
After WWI, Minpon Shugi became the ideology of the Taishō Democracy Movement. The Taishō Democracy Movement was led by activists who were inspired by the Minpon Shugi that Yoshino advocated.<ref>{{Cite book|last1=Mitani, Taichirō|title=Taishō demokurashī-ron : Yoshino Sakuzō no jidai|last2=三谷太一郎|date=15 August 2013|isbn=978-4-13-030157-2|edition=Daisanhan|location=Tōkyō|pages=125|oclc=856900297}}</ref>
=== National discontent during the war === The Russo-Japanese War caused serious damage to Japan's finances. Japan used foreign debt to start the war. It is said that the reason why Japan barely won was that the Russian government held back its main force for fear of revolution. Japanese soldiers were plagued by Russian [[:en:machine gun|machine gun]]s and even some who survived were covered all over with wounds. The peace treaty offered by U.S. [[Theodore Roosevelt|President Roosevelt]] achieved "more than expected," said a government-leaning newspaper at the time.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Shinobu|first=Seizaburō|title=大正デモクラシー史|publisher=NIPPON HYORON SHA|year=1978|location=Tokyo|pages=14}}</ref>
On the other hand, the public reaction was the exact opposite. The people had sacrificed a great deal for the Russo-Japanese War. The government had imposed higher taxes on the citizens to pay for the war, and the peasants had had to pay most of their profits in taxes. The tax also dealt a blow to small and medium-sized businesses, and even during the war, the nation's silk cloth manufacturers' associations, wholesale brokers, union representatives, and the retail trade association came to Tokyo to oppose it. The same kind of opposition arose towards the salt monopoly.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Matsuo|first=Takayoshi|title=Taishō democracy|publisher=Iwanami Shoten|year=1994|isbn=978-4006000554|location=Tokyo|pages=29}}</ref> Thus, dissatisfaction with the war grew from rural areas across the country to urban areas.
== Effects of Taishō Democracy == The nation's trend towards universal democracy and independence can be seen as products of Taishō Democracy. These effects can be seen in various fields in that era, for example:
=== Politics === People started to express more of their opinions about the nation's decisions. In other words, people began to think for themselves, beyond what they were told by national leadership, and tried to exert political power to express their opinions. Political movements such as launching party and the movement for universal suffrage are some examples.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|last1=Mitani, Taichirō|title=Taishō demokurashī-ron : Yoshino Sakuzō no jidai|last2=三谷太一郎|date=15 August 2013|isbn=978-4-13-030157-2|edition=Daisanhan|location=Tōkyō|pages=1|oclc=856900297}}</ref>
=== Economy === The tendency of independence of the capital occurred and people sought to be free from the regulations. In other words, people demanded a [[:en:limited government|limited government]]. As a result, the power of capitalists or finance experts got stronger than before.<ref name=":1" /> Moreover, people began the movement of abolition of tax and claimed a disarmament.<ref>{{Cite book|last1=Mitani, Taichirō|title=Taishō demokurashī-ron : Yoshino Sakuzō no jidai|last2=三谷太一郎|date=15 August 2013|isbn=978-4-13-030157-2|edition=Daisanhan|location=Tōkyō|pages=12|oclc=856900297}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|last1=Matsuo, Takayoshi, 1929-2014.|title=Taishō demokurashī|last2=松尾尊兌, 1929-2014.|date=1994|publisher=Iwanami Shoten|isbn=4-00-260184-6|location=Tōkyō|pages=1|oclc=31151962}}</ref>
=== Education === Education for its own sake, independent of other concerns, was also advocated. University leadership and the press became independent of government, and people gained the ability to express their own opinions, independent from government views.<ref>{{Cite book|last1=Mitani, Taichirō|title=Taishō demokurashī-ron : Yoshino Sakuzō no jidai|last2=三谷太一郎|date=15 August 2013|isbn=978-4-13-030157-2|edition=Daisanhan|location=Tōkyō|pages=2|oclc=856900297}}</ref> Moreover, the increase in the literacy rate allowed people to read more books and get more information.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Nihon no kin-gendaishi o dō miru ka|date=2010|publisher=Iwanami Shoten|others=Iwanami Shoten. Iwanami Shinsho Henshūbu., 岩波書店. 岩波新書編集部.|isbn=978-4-00-431051-8|location=Tōkyō|pages=84|oclc=534643529}}</ref>
=== Women's Activists === The social system of patriarchy still existed in Japan at that time; however, there had been a gradual change to the traditional position of women. [[:en:Itō Noe|Itō Noe]], a feminist and editor of the magazine [[:en:Bluestocking (magazine)|''Seitō'']] ("Bluestocking"), complained that women did not have the same rights as men. Itō Noe, along with other women's activists such as [[:en:Hiratsuka Raichō|Hiratsuka Raichō]] and [[:en:Nobuko Yoshiya|Yoshiya Nobuko]], tried to make changes to the system of [[:en:male privilege|male privilege]], and aimed for the independence of women.<ref>{{Cite book |title=日本の近現代史をどう見るか (Nihon no kin-gendaishi o dō miru ka; "How to view Japan's modern history") |date=2010 |publisher=岩波新書 (Iwanami Shinsho) |isbn=978-4-00-431051-8 |location=Tōkyō |pages=88–89 |oclc=534643529}}</ref>
== See also ==
* {{ill|Keishichi Hirasawa|ja|平澤計七|WD=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Keishichi Hirasawa|qq=}} (平澤計七) * [[:en:Yamamoto Senji|Senji Yamamoto]] (山本宣治) * [[Takiji Kobayashi]] (小林多喜二) * [[:en:Hiratsuka Raichō|Hiratsuka Raichō]] (平塚らいてう) * [[:en:Itō Noe|Itō Noe]] (伊藤野枝) * [[:en:Nobuko Yoshiya|Yoshiya Nobuko]] (吉屋信子) * [[Women's suffrage in Japan|Woman's suffrage]] (女性参政権) * [[:en:Minpon Shugi|Minpon Shugi]] (民本主義) * [[:en:Universal suffrage|Universal suffrage]] * [[:en:Freedom and People's Rights Movement|Freedom and People's Rights Movement]] (自由民権運動) * [[:en:Hanshinkan Modernism|Hanshinkan Modernism]]
== References == <references />
== External links == * https://www.facinghistory.org/nanjing-atrocities/nation-building/taisho-democracy-japan-1912-1926 * https://www.japantimes.co.jp/life/2012/07/29/general/taisho-democracy-pays-the-ultimate-price/#.XulryUX7Ryw
